en.extract:
Austen's brown hornbill (Anorrhinus austeni), also known as the white-throated brown hornbill or brown hornbill, is a medium-sized species of hornbill found in forests from northeastern India to Vietnam and northern Thailand. It is rare throughout its range and is threatened by habitat loss and fragmentation.


Description
Austen's brown hornbill has dark brown upperparts (back, wings, and tail) with a lighter-coloured belly, cheeks, and throat. It has a white-tipped tail and wings and has blue-coloured bare skin around the eyes. The species is sexually dimorphic. The bill and casque are ivory white in females and cream-coloured in males. Females have a grey-brown throat, belly, and cheeks, whereas males have a whitish throat and cheeks with a reddish belly. The female also has a yellow wash below the eye. Juveniles look similar to adult males but have a short yellow bill, orange skin around the eye, and pale-brown feather tips. The male weighs 710–900 g (25–32 oz) and is 73–80 cm (29–31 in) long. The female is smaller.


Taxonomy
Thomas C. Jerdon formally described Austen's brown hornbill as Anorrhinus austeni in 1872 in Asalu, Cachar Hills, Assam, India in The Ibis. The genus name comes from the Greek prefix ano- meaning above and rhinus meaning nose. The species is thought to be named after the naturalist Henry Haversham Godwin-Austen.
Austen's brown hornbill was once categorized as a subspecies of Tickell's brown hornbill and both Austen's and Tickell's brown hornbills were in the genus Ptilolaemus. The subspecies classification of Austen's brown hornbill was the accepted taxonomy from 1945–2003 but more recent genetic studies have placed Austen's brown hornbill and Tickell's brown hornbill as sister species both in the genus Anorrhinus. The bushy-crested hornbill is the only other species in the Anorrhinus genus.
The population in Thailand and Indochina is sometimes treated as a race called indochinensis.


Distribution and habitat
Austen's brown hornbill is found in northeastern India, central and northern Thailand, Myanmar, Laos, Vietnam, Cambodia, and historically China but there have been no recent reports. It is rare in most of its range and found mainly in national parks and sanctuaries where forests are protected such as the Patkai Wildlife Sanctuary and Namdapha Tiger Reserve. It is common in Laos but the population is declining.
It lives mainly in dense evergreen forests in lowlands and foothills, and is less commonly found in deciduous forests. It has been observed up to 1,000 m (3,300 ft) in India, 1,500 m (4,900 ft) in Southeast Asia, and 1,800 m (5,900 ft) in Tibet. It moves through the forest at or below canopy level, but uses all canopy layers equally.


Behaviour


Vocalizations
Austen's brown hornbill is a noisy bird. It makes loud screams, croaks, chisels, low cackles, squeals, and a nasal "ank-ank-ank" call. It sounds similar to the Malabar pied hornbill and the bushy-crested hornbill but less harsh. It often does noisy contact calling when feeding.


Diet

Austen's brown hornbill eats fruits and small animals. The percentage of animal matter in its diet ranges from 40–79% and includes arthropods, molluscs, earthworms, reptiles, bats, and bird eggs and chicks. The percentage of fruit matter ranges from 20–60% with a large portion of that being figs. Compared to other hornbill species, Austen's brown hornbill is an animal specialist and spends more time hunting animals below the canopy. The proportion of various food items and amount of food consumed overall changes throughout the phases of the breeding season.


Social groups
They live in groups of about 2-15 birds, on average 6, but up to 80 birds have been seen roosting together. These groups are territorial and sedentary with small home ranges of 4.3–5.9 km2 (1.7–2.3 sq mi). Non-breeding offspring remain in a social group with their parents.


Reproduction
Austen's brown hornbill is a monogamous, cooperative breeder. A dominant male-female breeding pair has an average of two helpers but the number of helpers can range from zero to five. The helpers are the non-breeding offspring of the pair and are mostly adult males but sometimes juveniles. Helpers bring food to the female and chicks and defend the nest from predators, birds of the same species, and other closely related species. Food is delivered by regurgitation. Austen's brown hornbill nests in natural tree cavities or old holes made by woodpeckers about 5–7 m (16–23 ft) off the ground, sometimes up to 27 m (89 ft). Tree species used for nesting include magnolia, hollong, Anthoshorea assamica, and Artocarpus chama. Breeding is initiated in February–March depending on location, usually before the monsoon period. The main breeding season coincides with the peak availability of non-fig fruits. Like other hornbills, the female seals herself in the nesting cavity and moults her flight feathers. The average clutch size is two, rarely up to five. The incubation period is 24–30 days and the nesting period is 57–62 days. The female and chicks fledge at the same time in May–July depending on location. The whole nesting cycle lasts 73–112 days.


Threats and conservation
Austen's brown hornbill is listed as Near Threatened on the IUCN red list of threatened species. It faces threats from logging, expanding roads and settlements, and hunting. It is hunted by several tribes for the caseque, meat, fat and feathers. While hunting is not usually done during the breeding season, winter hunting is still a threat to Austen's brown hornbill populations. There is evidence that Austen's brown hornbill can live in logged forests but nesting attempts are less successful. Human disruption disproportionately affects Austen's brown hornbill, with populations mainly found in protected areas. The probability of habitat use outside of protected areas is lower in Austen's brown hornbill than other hornbills in the same habitat range.
The IUCN has put forth recommendations for the conservation of Austen's brown hornbill. They suggest conducting new surveys in areas like Myanmar and Laos where population sizes are less known, and repeating surveys across the distribution to monitor the change in population size. To decrease hunting pressure, they suggest raising awareness of the conservation status of Austen's brown hornbill. Additionally, they suggest studies to determine habitat requirements to inform protected area designation.


References


External links
Austen's brown hornbill photo gallery on eBird
Austen's brown hornbill sound recordings on xeno-canto

'''Austen's brown hornbill''' ('''''Anorrhinus austeni'''''), also known as the '''white-throated brown hornbill''' or '''brown hornbill,''' is a medium-sized species of [[hornbill]] found in forests from northeastern [[India]] to [[Vietnam]] and northern [[Thailand]]. It is rare throughout its range and is threatened by habitat loss and fragmentation.

== Description ==
Austen's brown hornbill has dark brown upperparts (back, wings, and tail) with a lighter-coloured belly, cheeks, and throat. It has a white-tipped tail and wings and has blue-coloured bare skin around the eyes.<ref name=":2" /> The species is [[Sexual dimorphism|sexually dimorphic]]. The bill and [[Casque (anatomy)|casque]] are ivory white in females and cream-coloured in males. Females have a grey-brown throat, belly, and cheeks,<ref name=":2" /> whereas males have a whitish throat and cheeks with a reddish belly. The female also has a yellow wash below the eye. Juveniles look similar to adult males but have a short yellow bill, orange skin around the eye, and pale-brown feather tips. The male weighs {{cvt|710-900|g}} and is {{cvt|73-80|cm}} long.<ref name=":0" /> The female is smaller.

== Taxonomy ==
[[Thomas C. Jerdon]] formally described Austen's brown hornbill as ''Anorrhinus austeni'' in 1872 in Asalu, [[Dima Hasao district|Cachar Hills, Assam]], India in [[Ibis (journal)|The Ibis.]]<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Salvin |first=Osbert |date=1872 |title=Dr. T. C. Jerdon's Supplementary Notes |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/8617977 |journal=The Ibis |volume=2 |issue=3 |page=6}}</ref> The genus name comes from the Greek prefix ano- meaning above<ref>{{Cite dictionary |title=Definition of ANO- |url=https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/ano- |access-date=2025-11-08 |encyclopedia=Merriam-Webster |language=en}}</ref> and rhinus meaning nose.<ref>{{Cite dictionary |title=Definition of -RHINUS |url=https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/-rhinus |access-date=2025-11-08 |encyclopedia=Merriam-Webster |language=en}}</ref> The species is thought to be named after the naturalist [[Henry Haversham Godwin-Austen]].

Austen's brown hornbill was once categorized as a subspecies of [[Tickell's brown hornbill]] and both Austen's and Tickell's brown hornbills were in the genus ''Ptilolaemus''.<ref name=":5">{{Cite journal |last=Gonzalez |first=Juan-Carlos T. |last2=Sheldon |first2=Ben C. |last3=Collar |first3=Nigel J. |last4=Tobias |first4=Joseph A. |date=2013-05-01 |title=A comprehensive molecular phylogeny for the hornbills (Aves: Bucerotidae) |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1055790313000699 |journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution |volume=67 |issue=2 |pages=468–483 |doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2013.02.012 |issn=1055-7903|url-access=subscription }}</ref> The subspecies classification of Austen's brown hornbill was the accepted taxonomy from 1945<ref>{{Cite book |last=Peters |first=James Lee |title=Check-list of birds of the world |last2=Traylor |first2=Melvin Alvah |last3=Mayr |first3=Ernst |last4=Greenway |first4=James Cowan |last5=Paynter |first5=Raymond A. |last6=Cottrell |first6=G. William |publisher=Harvard University Press |year=1945 |edition=5 |language=en}}</ref>–2003<ref>{{Cite book |last=Dickinson |first=E.C. |title=The Howard and Moore Complete Checklist of Birds of the World |last2=Bahr |first2=N. |last3=Dowsett |first3=R. |last4=Pearson |first4=D. |last5=Remsen |first5=V. |last6=Roselaar |first6=C.S. |last7=Schodde |first7=D. |publisher=Londen: A & C Black |year=2004 |language=en |hdl=hdl.handle.net/11245/1.225888}}</ref> but more recent genetic studies<ref name=":5" /> have placed Austen's brown hornbill and Tickell's brown hornbill as sister species both in the genus ''Anorrhinus''. The [[bushy-crested hornbill]] is the only other species in the ''Anorrhinus'' genus.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Gonzalez |first=Juan-Carlos T. |last2=Sheldon |first2=Ben C. |last3=Tobias |first3=Joseph A. |date=2013-10-07 |title=Environmental stability and the evolution of cooperative breeding in hornbills |url=https://royalsocietypublishing.org/doi/10.1098/rspb.2013.1297 |journal=Proceedings of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences |volume=280 |issue=1768 |article-number=20131297 |doi=10.1098/rspb.2013.1297 |pmc=3757966 |pmid=23926149}}</ref>

The population in Thailand and [[Indochina]] is sometimes treated as a race called ''indochinensis''.<ref name=":0" />

== Distribution and habitat ==
Austen's brown hornbill is found in northeastern [[India]], central and northern [[Thailand]], [[Myanmar]], [[Laos]], [[Vietnam]], [[Cambodia]], and historically [[China]] but there have been no recent reports.<ref name=iucn/> It is rare in most of its range and found mainly in national parks and sanctuaries where forests are protected such as the [[Dehing Patkai National Park|Patkai Wildlife Sanctuary]] and [[Namdapha National Park|Namdapha Tiger Reserve]]. It is common in Laos but the population is declining.<ref name=iucn/>

It lives mainly in dense evergreen forests in lowlands and foothills,<ref name=iucn/> and is less commonly found in deciduous forests.<ref name=":2" /> It has been observed up to {{convert|1000|m|ft|abbr=on}} in India, {{convert|1500|m|ft|abbr=on}} in Southeast Asia, and {{convert|1800|m|ft|abbr=on}} in Tibet.<ref name=":0" /> It moves through the forest at or below canopy level,<ref name=":0" /> but uses all canopy layers equally.<ref name=":4" />

== Behaviour ==

=== Vocalizations ===
Austen's brown hornbill is a noisy bird. It makes loud screams, croaks, chisels, low cackles,<ref name=":2" /> squeals,<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Hussain |first=Mohommad Miraj |last2=Gogoi |first2=Monsoon Jyoti |last3=Deori |first3=Biswajyoti |last4=Singha |first4=Hilloljyoti |date=2015 |title=Austen's Brown Hornbill Anorrhinus austeniin Barail Wildlife Sanctuary, Assam, India |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/293027072_Austen%27s_Brown_Hornbill_Anorrhinus_austeni_in_Barail_Wildlife_Sanctuary_Assam_India |journal=BirdingASIA |volume=24 |pages=126–127 |via=ResearchGate}}</ref> and a nasal "ank-ank-ank" call.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Deignan |first=H. G. |title=The birds of northern Thailand |publisher=Bulletin of the United States national Museum |year=1945 |pages=220–221 |language=en}}</ref> It sounds similar to the [[Malabar pied hornbill]]<ref name=":2" /> and the [[bushy-crested hornbill]]<ref name=":0" /> but less harsh. It often does noisy [[contact call]]ing when feeding.<ref name=":2" />

=== Diet ===
[[File:Austen's Brown Hornbill.jpg|thumb|Group in Namdapha National Park, India]]
Austen's brown hornbill eats fruits and small animals. The percentage of animal matter in its diet ranges from 40–79%<ref name=":0">{{Cite book |last=Ponsawat |first=Philai |title=Hornbills of the world: a photographic guide |publisher=Singapore : Draco Publishing and Distribution Pte Ltd; Bangkok : Hornbill Research Foundation |year=2013 |isbn=978-981-07-3528-9 |pages=158–161 |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last=Bora |first=Bhaskar |last2=Sonowal |first2=Dhruba |last3=Khakhlari |first3=Nayan |last4=Datta |first4=Aparajita |date=2023 |title=The breeding biology of the cooperatively breeding White-throated Brown Hornbill (Anorrhinus austeni) |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/373048367_The_breeding_biology_of_the_cooperatively_breeding_White-throated_Brown_Hornbill_Anorrhinus_austeni |journal=Hornbill Nat. Hist. & Conserv. |volume=4 |pages=1–19 |via=ResearchGate}}</ref> and includes [[arthropod]]s, [[Mollusca|molluscs]], earthworms, reptiles, bats, and bird eggs and chicks.<ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last=Kemp |first=Alan |title=The hornbills: Bucerotiformes |publisher=Oxford; New York : Oxford University Press |year=1995 |isbn= |pages=101–104 |language=en}}</ref> The percentage of fruit matter ranges from 20–60%<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> with a large portion of that being figs. Compared to other hornbill species, Austen's brown hornbill is an animal specialist and spends more time hunting animals below the canopy.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Poonswad |first=Pilai |last2=Tsuji |first2=Atsuo |last3=Jirawatkavi |first3=Narong |date=2004 |title=Estimation of nutrients delivered to nest inmates by four sympatric species of hornbills in Khao Yai National Park, Thailand |url=https://www.jstage.jst.go.jp/article/osj/3/2/3_2_99/_article |journal=Ornithological Science |volume=3 |issue=2 |pages=99–112 |doi=10.2326/osj.3.99|doi-access=free }}</ref> The proportion of various food items and amount of food consumed overall changes throughout the phases of the breeding season.<ref name=":1" />

=== Social groups ===
They live in groups of about 2-15 birds, on average 6,<ref name=":4" /> but up to 80 birds have been seen roosting together.<ref name=":0" /> These groups are territorial and sedentary with small home ranges of {{convert|4.3-5.9|km2|sqmi|abbr=on}}.<ref name=":0" /> Non-breeding offspring remain in a social group with their parents.<ref name=":1" />

=== Reproduction ===
Austen's brown hornbill is a monogamous, [[Cooperative breeding|cooperative breeder]]. A dominant male-female breeding pair has an average of two [[Helpers at the nest|helpers]] but the number of helpers can range from zero to five.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> The helpers are the non-breeding offspring of the pair and are mostly adult males but sometimes juveniles.<ref name=":1" /> Helpers bring food to the female and chicks and defend the nest from predators, birds of the same species, and other closely related species.<ref name=":2" /> Food is delivered by regurgitation.<ref name=":4">{{Cite journal |last=Datta |first=Aparajita |date=2009 |title=Observations on Rufous-necked Aceros nipalensis and Austen's Brown Anorrhinus austeni Hornbills in Arunachal Pradesh: natural history, conservation status, and threats |url=https://indianbirds.in/pdfs/IB.5.4_108-117.pdf |journal=Indian Birds |volume=5 |issue=4 |pages=108–117}}</ref> Austen's brown hornbill nests in natural tree cavities or old holes made by woodpeckers about {{convert|5-7|m|ft|abbr=on}}<ref name=":1" /> off the ground, sometimes up to {{convert|27|m|ft|abbr=on}}.<ref name=":0" /> Tree species used for nesting include [[magnolia]], [[Dipterocarpus retusus|hollong]], ''[[Anthoshorea assamica]]'', and ''[[Artocarpus chama]].''<ref name=":4" /> Breeding is initiated in February–March<ref name=":2" /> depending on location, usually before the monsoon period.<ref name=":1" /> The main breeding season coincides with the peak availability of non-fig fruits.<ref name=":1" /> Like other hornbills, the female seals herself in the nesting cavity<ref name=":1" /> and [[Moulting|moults]] her flight feathers.<ref name=":0" /> The average [[Clutch (eggs)|clutch]] size is two,<ref name=":1" /> rarely up to five.<ref name=":0" /> The incubation period is 24–30 days and the nesting period is 57–62 days.<ref name=":1" /> The female and chicks [[fledge]] at the same time in May–July<ref name=":2" /><ref name=":1" /> depending on location. The whole nesting cycle lasts 73–112 days.<ref name=":0" />

== Threats and conservation ==
Austen's brown hornbill is listed as Near Threatened on the [[IUCN Red List|IUCN red list of threatened species]].<ref name=iucn/> It faces threats from logging, expanding roads and settlements, and hunting. It is hunted by several tribes for the caseque, meat, fat and feathers. While hunting is not usually done during the breeding season, winter hunting is still a threat to Austen's brown hornbill populations. There is evidence that Austen's brown hornbill can live in logged forests but nesting attempts are less successful.<ref name=":4" /> Human disruption disproportionately affects Austen's brown hornbill, with populations mainly found in protected areas. The probability of habitat use outside of protected areas is lower in Austen's brown hornbill than other hornbills in the same habitat range.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Naniwadekar |first=Rohit |last2=Mishra |first2=Charudutt |last3=Isvaran |first3=Kavita |last4=Madhusudan |first4=M. D. |last5=Datta |first5=Aparajita |date=2015 |title=Looking beyond parks: the conservation value of unprotected areas for hornbills in Arunachal Pradesh, Eastern Himalaya |url=https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/oryx/article/looking-beyond-parks-the-conservation-value-of-unprotected-areas-for-hornbills-in-arunachal-pradesh-eastern-himalaya/C4A9A4CA382DE78389C8070A9593F05C |journal=Oryx |language=en |volume=49 |issue=2 |pages=303–311 |doi=10.1017/S0030605313000781 |issn=0030-6053}}</ref>

The [[International Union for Conservation of Nature|IUCN]] has put forth recommendations for the conservation of Austen's brown hornbill. They suggest conducting new surveys in areas like Myanmar and Laos where population sizes are less known, and repeating surveys across the distribution to monitor the change in population size. To decrease hunting pressure, they suggest raising awareness of the conservation status of Austen's brown hornbill. Additionally, they suggest studies to determine habitat requirements to inform protected area designation.<ref name=iucn/>

zh.extract:
白喉犀鳥（學名：Anorrhinus austeni，英文：Austen's Brown Hornbill），犀鸟科鳳頭犀鳥屬，體長約880毫米，成體背部為深棕灰色，腹部為淺棕色；喉部白色，飛翔時可看到翅的末端羽毛和尾尖兩邊的尾羽為白色。次級飛羽的下半段為淡黃色，羽上有白色和淡黃色斑點。


雌雄辨別
雌鳥的喙為暗棕色，雄鳥則為黃色。


主要分布
印度、泰國、越南、中國等地。
